---
title: "Structural Priming Suite: Mixed-Effects Analysis Plan"
author: "Geometry of Syntax"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: '3'
params:
  repo_root: '.'
  input_dir: behavioral_results/analysis_ready
  output_dir: behavioral_results/r_mixed_models
  item_level_path: item_level_master.csv
  baseline_shifts_path: baseline_centered_logodds_shifts.csv
  roi_long_path: roi_pe_item_level.csv
  roi_percentage_path: roi_pe_percentage_summary.csv
  ife_path: ife_prime_surprisal_item_level.csv
  exp2_generation_path: exp2_generation_item_level.csv
  exp4_comprehension_path: exp4_comprehension_item_level.csv
  exp4_pe_path: exp4_sinclair_pe_item_level.csv
  model_condition_reference: 774M_base
  model_size_reference: 774M
  lexicality_reference: core
  baseline_reference: no_prime
  run_sensitivity_models: true
  save_model_objects: false
---

# Purpose

This notebook is the analysis scaffold for the active/passive structural priming suite. It is organized by **research question**, not by experiment. The same logic is then applied to whichever experiments and models are present in the analysis-ready CSVs.

The notebook assumes that Python-side scripts have already exported tidy, analysis-ready files. If a file is missing, the relevant section is skipped with a clear message rather than failing the whole notebook.

To regenerate the current analysis-ready CSVs from the available result folders:

``` bash
python3 scripts/analysis/49_prepare_analysis_ready_data.py
```

Primary analysis principle:

- report baseline active/passive preference separately from prime-induced shift;
- use `model_condition` as the primary model comparison variable, so base and instruction-tuned models are not accidentally pooled;
- treat `model_size`, `model_family`, and instruction tuning as descriptive or sensitivity-only metadata unless explicitly modeled;
- include item/order/token controls wherever available;
- use item-level random intercepts where repeated target items are observed.

Scoring conventions for Experiments 1a-4 are documented in `docs/experiment_scoring_methods.md`. In particular, `preference_total` is active-minus-passive for teacher-forced active/passive choice files, while `passive_minus_active_*` columns use the passive-centered sign.

# Setup

```{r setup}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = TRUE
)

required_pkgs <- c(
  "readr", "dplyr", "tidyr", "purrr", "stringr", "forcats",
  "ggplot2", "tibble", "glue", "knitr",
  "lme4", "emmeans", "broom.mixed", "performance"
)

missing_pkgs <- required_pkgs[
  !vapply(required_pkgs, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)
]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  stop("Install these packages before knitting: ", paste(missing_pkgs, collapse = ", "))
}

invisible(lapply(required_pkgs, library, character.only = TRUE))

resolve_repo_root <- function(param_root) {
  candidates <- unique(c(
    as.character(param_root),
    ".",
    "..",
    normalizePath(file.path(getwd(), ".."), mustWork = FALSE)
  ))

  for (candidate in candidates) {
    normalized <- normalizePath(candidate, mustWork = FALSE)
    if (file.exists(file.path(normalized, "behavioral_results")) &&
        file.exists(file.path(normalized, "scripts"))) {
      return(normalized)
    }
  }

  normalizePath(as.character(param_root), mustWork = FALSE)
}

repo_root <- resolve_repo_root(params$repo_root)
input_dir <- file.path(repo_root, params$input_dir)
output_dir <- file.path(repo_root, params$output_dir)
dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

set.seed(13)

theme_set(ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
```

# Helpers

```{r helpers}
path_from_param <- function(param_value) {
  path <- as.character(param_value)
  if (grepl("^(/|[A-Za-z]:[/\\\\])", path)) {
    return(path)
  }
  file.path(input_dir, path)
}
```

```{r helper-functions}

# Loads files safely without crashing if optional CSVs are missing.
# Standardizes column names/labels, especially prime labels.
# Creates default metadata columns if they are absent.
# Creates z-scored numeric controls for item order and token-length differences.
# Builds and fits mixed models safely, then saves summaries.

read_optional_csv <- function(path, label) {
  if (!file.exists(path)) {
    message(glue::glue("Skipping {label}; missing file: {path}"))
    return(NULL)
  }
  readr::read_csv(path, show_col_types = FALSE)
}

has_cols <- function(df, cols) {
  !is.null(df) && all(cols %in% names(df))
}

require_cols <- function(df, cols, label) {
  missing <- setdiff(cols, names(df))
  if (length(missing) > 0) {
    stop(glue::glue("{label} is missing required columns: {paste(missing, collapse = ', ')}"))
  }
  invisible(df)
}

first_present <- function(df, candidates) {
  found <- candidates[candidates %in% names(df)]
  if (length(found) == 0) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  found[[1]]
}

z_if_present <- function(df, col) {
  if (!col %in% names(df)) {
    return(df)
  }
  out <- df
  new_col <- paste0(col, "_z")
  out[[new_col]] <- as.numeric(scale(out[[col]]))
  out
}

coalesce_missing_col <- function(df, col, value) {
  if (!col %in% names(df)) {
    df[[col]] <- value
  }
  df
}

canonicalize_common <- function(df) {
  if (is.null(df)) {
    return(NULL)
  }

  out <- df %>%
    mutate(
      prime_condition = if ("prime_condition" %in% names(.)) {
        stringr::str_replace_all(as.character(prime_condition), c(
          "active_prime" = "active",
          "passive_prime" = "passive",
          "filler_prime" = "filler",
          "no_prime_eos" = "no_prime",
          "no_prime_empty" = "no_prime",
          "no_demo" = "no_prime"
        ))
      } else {
        NA_character_
      }
    )

  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "role_order", "not_applicable")
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "task_type", "unspecified")
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "model_size", "unspecified")
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "model_family", "unspecified")
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "model_instruct", "unspecified")
  if (!"model_condition" %in% names(out)) {
    out <- out %>%
      mutate(model_condition = paste(model_size, model_instruct, sep = "_"))
  }
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "lexicality_condition", "unspecified")
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "item_id", NA_character_)
  out <- coalesce_missing_col(out, "item_index", NA_integer_)

  out %>%
    mutate(
      prime_condition = factor(
        prime_condition,
        levels = c("no_prime", "filler", "active", "passive")
      ),
      lexicality_condition = forcats::fct_relevel(
        factor(lexicality_condition),
        params$lexicality_reference
      ),
      model_size = forcats::fct_relevel(
        factor(model_size),
        params$model_size_reference
      ),
      model_condition = forcats::fct_relevel(
        factor(model_condition),
        params$model_condition_reference
      ),
      model_family = factor(model_family),
      model_instruct = factor(model_instruct),
      task_type = factor(task_type),
      role_order = factor(role_order),
      item_factor = factor(ifelse(is.na(item_id), item_index, item_id))
    ) %>%
    z_if_present("item_order") %>%
    z_if_present("item_index") %>%
    z_if_present("token_count_diff") %>%
    z_if_present("passive_minus_active_token_count") %>%
    z_if_present("prime_token_count_diff") %>%
    z_if_present("target_token_count_diff")
}

available_controls <- function(df, candidates) {
  candidates[candidates %in% names(df)]
}

random_item_term <- function(df) {
  if ("item_factor" %in% names(df) && dplyr::n_distinct(df$item_factor, na.rm = TRUE) > 1) {
    "(1 | item_factor)"
  } else {
    NULL
  }
}

build_formula <- function(response, fixed_terms, random_terms = NULL) {
  fixed_terms <- fixed_terms[!is.na(fixed_terms) & nzchar(fixed_terms)]
  random_terms <- random_terms[!is.na(random_terms) & nzchar(random_terms)]
  rhs <- c(fixed_terms, random_terms)
  stats::as.formula(paste(response, "~", paste(rhs, collapse = " + ")))
}

fit_lmer_safe <- function(formula, data, label) {
  message(glue::glue("Fitting LMM: {label}"))
  message(format(formula))
  tryCatch(
    lme4::lmer(
      formula,
      data = data,
      REML = FALSE,
      control = lme4::lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5))
    ),
    error = function(e) {
      warning(glue::glue("Model failed [{label}]: {conditionMessage(e)}"))
      NULL
    }
  )
}

fit_glmer_safe <- function(formula, data, label) {
  message(glue::glue("Fitting GLMM: {label}"))
  message(format(formula))
  tryCatch(
    lme4::glmer(
      formula,
      data = data,
      family = binomial,
      control = lme4::glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5))
    ),
    error = function(e) {
      warning(glue::glue("Model failed [{label}]: {conditionMessage(e)}"))
      NULL
    }
  )
}

tidy_model <- function(model, model_id) {
  if (is.null(model)) {
    return(tibble::tibble())
  }
  broom.mixed::tidy(model, effects = "fixed", conf.int = TRUE) %>%
    mutate(model_id = model_id, .before = 1)
}

save_model_summary <- function(model, model_id) {
  if (is.null(model)) {
    return(invisible(NULL))
  }
  txt_path <- file.path(output_dir, paste0(model_id, "_summary.txt"))
  capture.output(summary(model), file = txt_path)

  if (isTRUE(params$save_model_objects)) {
    saveRDS(model, file.path(output_dir, paste0(model_id, ".rds")))
  }
  invisible(NULL)
}

write_model_table <- function(rows, filename) {
  table <- dplyr::bind_rows(rows)
  if (nrow(table) > 0) {
    readr::write_csv(table, file.path(output_dir, filename))
  }
  table
}
```

# Load Data

Expected analysis-ready files:

- `item_level_master.csv`: item-level log-probability and/or choice measures.
- `baseline_centered_logodds_shifts.csv`: one row per item/model/baseline-centered shift.
- `roi_pe_item_level.csv`: long ROI-level PE table.
- `roi_pe_percentage_summary.csv`: region percentage summaries.
- `ife_prime_surprisal_item_level.csv`: IFE-style surprisal and target shifts.
- `exp2_generation_item_level.csv`: generation labels for Experiment 2, if not included in master.
- `exp4_comprehension_item_level.csv`: comprehension/role-recovery accuracy for Experiment 4, if not included in master.
- `exp4_sinclair_pe_item_level.csv`: Experiment 4 target-processing PE, exported separately because it is already contrast-level rather than prime-condition-level.

```{r load-data}
item_level <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$item_level_path), "item-level master") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

baseline_shifts <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$baseline_shifts_path), "baseline-centered shifts") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

roi_long <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$roi_long_path), "ROI item-level PE") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

roi_percentage <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$roi_percentage_path), "ROI percentage summary")

ife_item <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$ife_path), "IFE item-level") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

exp2_generation <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$exp2_generation_path), "Experiment 2 generation item-level") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

exp4_comprehension <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$exp4_comprehension_path), "Experiment 4 comprehension item-level") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

exp4_pe <- read_optional_csv(path_from_param(params$exp4_pe_path), "Experiment 4 Sinclair PE item-level") %>%
  canonicalize_common()

token_level <- readr::read_csv(
  file.path(input_dir, "token_level_probabilities.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

token_roi_summary <- readr::read_csv(
  file.path(input_dir, "token_roi_summary.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

```

```{r}
token_level %>%
  dplyr::count(
    experiment,
    task_type,
    lexicality_condition,
    model_condition,
    prime_condition,
    candidate_voice,
    roi
  )

```

# Design Checks

```{r design-checks}
describe_table <- function(df, label) {
  if (is.null(df)) {
    return(invisible(NULL))
  }
  cat("\n\n##", label, "\n")
  print(dim(df))
  print(names(df))
}

describe_table(item_level, "item_level")
describe_table(baseline_shifts, "baseline_shifts")
describe_table(roi_long, "roi_long")
describe_table(ife_item, "ife_item")
describe_table(exp2_generation, "exp2_generation")
describe_table(exp4_comprehension, "exp4_comprehension")
describe_table(exp4_pe, "exp4_pe")
```

```{r balance-checks}
balance_table <- function(df, label, cols) {
  if (is.null(df)) {
    message(glue::glue("Skipping {label}; data frame is missing."))
    return(invisible(NULL))
  }

  missing <- setdiff(cols, names(df))
  if (length(missing) > 0) {
    message(glue::glue(
      "Skipping {label}; missing columns: {paste(missing, collapse = ', ')}"
    ))
    return(invisible(NULL))
  }

  cat("\n\n##", label, "\n")
  df %>%
    count(across(all_of(cols))) %>%
    arrange(across(all_of(cols))) %>%
    knitr::kable()
}

balance_table(
  item_level,
  "Experiment 1b and 3 teacher-forced balance",
  c("experiment", "task_type", "lexicality_condition", "model_condition", "prime_condition", "target_voice")
)

balance_table(
  exp2_generation,
  "Experiment 2 generation input balance",
  c("experiment", "task_type", "dataset", "lexicality_condition", "model_condition", "prime_condition")
)

balance_table(
  exp2_generation,
  "Experiment 2 generation outcome counts",
  c("experiment", "dataset", "lexicality_condition", "model_condition", "prime_condition", "generation_voice")
)

balance_table(
  exp4_comprehension,
  "Experiment 4 comprehension balance",
  c("experiment", "task_type", "dataset", "lexicality_condition", "model_condition", "prime_condition", "target_voice", "question_type", "complexity_condition")
)

balance_table(
  exp4_pe,
  "Experiment 4 Sinclair PE balance",
  c("experiment", "task_type", "dataset", "lexicality_condition", "model_condition", "complexity_condition")
)
```

# EXPERIMENT 1

## RQ1: Baseline Active/Passive Bias

Premise: previous priming studies on LLMs found strong asymmetrical priming effects in favor of passive priming. No model-baseline has been established by previous studies.

RQ1.1: Before priming, do models prefer passive or active targets in a fixed-target scoring task? and does this vary by lexicality, and model type/size?

H1.1: In the no-prime baseline, models will show positive passive log-odds: logP(passive target) - logP(active target) \> 0 indicating that a passive target is preferrable even before any priming is introduced. This prediction is motivated by the possibility that passive targets are highly recoverable under incremental scoring because the passive frame contains overt, mutually predictive cues: auxiliary, participial morphology, and the by-phrase.

H1.2: Since Passive bias is driven by overt morphological cues, it will persist in jabberwocky because passive structure remains overtly marked by function words and morphological cues.

H1.3: Baseline passive bias will vary across model conditions, but we do not predict a monotonic effect of model size. Instruct models may show stronger passive bias if instruction tuning increases preference for formal, explanatory, and impersonal constructions, where passives are common. However, because SFT/RLHF data are not fully disclosed and instruction tuning also changes calibration, this prediction is exploratory.

Primary DV:

``` text
logodds_passive = logP(passive target) - logP(active target) -> for this exact same event, does the model assign more probability to the passive wording or the active wording?
```

Primary subset: `prime_condition == no_prime`. Filler baseline is a robustness check, not the main baseline.

logodds_passive \> 0 = passive preferred at baseline; logodds_passive \< 0 = active preferred at baseline; logodds_passive = 0 = no active/passive preference.

```{r}
exp1b_baseline <- item_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    prime_condition == "no_prime"
  )
```

```{r}
m_exp1b_baseline <- lmerTest::lmer(
  logodds_passive ~ lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_baseline,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp1b_baseline, test = "Chisq")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp1b_baseline)
```

- Intercept: llama-3B-base is passive-biased at baseline in the core condition
- lexicality: weaker passive bias in jabber than core for gpt-2-large; still positive tho.
- sentence lenght: difference in token length between passive and active targets strongly predicts logodds passive
- model: coefficients are positive for all models = all are passive-biased
- lexicality\*model: core-jabber difference varies by model but always positive = all are passive-biased

### Lexicality

```{r}
emm_exp1b_baseline <- emmeans(
  m_exp1b_baseline,
  ~ lexicality_condition * model_condition
)

test(emm_exp1b_baseline, null = 0)
```

```{r}
emm_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(emm_exp1b_baseline, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

lcl_col <- intersect(c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL", "lower.HPD"), names(emm_df))[1]
ucl_col <- intersect(c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL", "upper.HPD"), names(emm_df))[1]

ggplot(
  emm_df,
  aes(x = model_condition, y = emmean, color = lexicality_condition)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),
    size = 2.5
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = .data[[lcl_col]], ymax = .data[[ucl_col]]),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),
    width = 0.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1: no-prime baseline passive bias",
    x = "Model condition",
    y = "Passive log-odds",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

### Llama base vs Instruct

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(lmerTest)
library(emmeans)

exp1b_baseline_llama <- exp1b_baseline %>%
  filter(model_condition %in% c(
    "3B_base", "3B_instruct",
    "8B_base", "8B_instruct"
  )) %>%
  mutate(
    model_size = factor(model_size, levels = c("3B", "8B")),
    model_instruct = factor(model_instruct, levels = c("base", "instruct")),
    lexicality_condition = factor(lexicality_condition, levels = c("core", "jabberwocky"))
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp1b_baseline_llama <- lmerTest::lmer(
  logodds_passive ~ lexicality_condition * model_size * model_instruct +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_baseline_llama,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_exp1b_baseline_llama)
drop1(m_exp1b_baseline_llama, test = "F")
```

```{r}
emm_llama <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_baseline_llama,
  ~ lexicality_condition * model_size * model_instruct
)

contrast_llama <- pairs(
  emm_llama,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_size"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
contrast_llama
```

- core: 3B base is only marginally more passive-biased than 3B instruct. Difference is tiny.
- jabberwocky: 3B base is clearly more passive-biased than 3B instruct.
- core: 8B instruct is much more passive-biased than 8B base.
- jabberwocky: 8B instruct is much more passive-biased than 8B base.

### EXPL1.1: Token-count difference

```{r}
exp1b_baseline %>%
  ggplot(aes(
    x = passive_minus_active_token_count,
    y = logodds_passive,
    color = lexicality_condition
  )) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~ model_condition) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: token-count difference and passive bias",
    x = "Passive token count - active token count",
    y = "Passive log-odds",
    color = "Lexicality"
  )
```

- higher token-count difference predicts higher passive-log odds. Passive sentences are naturally longer than actives, for this reasons we controlled for lenght and used a mean difference, but those extra tokens are signature passive morphological elements.

H1B4: passive-roi (region of interest) is generating an incremental boost in passive logodds from the auxilliary and peaking at the by-phrase, even without any priming. To test this hypothesis we use token-level priming effect following Jumelet et al. 2024 work on datives.

```{r}
exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens <- token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    prime_condition == "no_prime",
    candidate_voice == "passive"
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

passive_roi_order <- c(
  "patient_np",
  "aux",
  "participle",
  "by",
  "agent_det",
  "agent_np",
  "punctuation"
)

exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens <- exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens %>%
  filter(roi %in% passive_roi_order) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = passive_roi_order)
  )

exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens <- token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    prime_condition == "no_prime",
    candidate_voice == "passive"
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

passive_roi_order <- c(
  "patient_np",
  "aux",
  "participle",
  "by",
  "agent_det",
  "agent_np"
)

exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens <- exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens %>%
  filter(roi %in% passive_roi_order) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = passive_roi_order),
    lexicality_condition = factor(lexicality_condition, levels = c("core", "jabberwocky"))
  ) %>%
  droplevels()
```

In passive targets, before any prime, which passive ROIs are more or less predictable?

```{r}
m_exp1b_passive_roi_baseline <- lmerTest::lmer(
  token_logprob ~ roi * lexicality_condition +
    (1 | item_id),
  data = exp1b_passive_baseline_tokens,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)


drop1(m_exp1b_passive_roi_baseline, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp1b_passive_roi_baseline)
```

- Content nouns are hard: patient NP and agent NP have low log-probability.
- Passive functional cues are easy: auxiliary, by, and agent determiner are much more predictable.
- The strongest predictability is in the by-phrase region, especially the determiner after by.
- Jabberwocky weakens lexical/content prediction but preserves the passive cue profile, especially at by and agent determiner.

```{r}
emm_roi <- emmeans(
  m_exp1b_passive_roi_baseline,
  ~ roi * lexicality_condition
)

emm_roi_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(emm_roi, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

passive_roi_order <- c(
  "patient_np",
  "aux",
  "participle",
  "by",
  "agent_det",
  "agent_np"
)

emm_roi_df <- emm_roi_df %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = passive_roi_order),
    lexicality_condition = factor(
      lexicality_condition,
      levels = c("core", "jabberwocky")
    )
  )
```

```{r}
ggplot(
  emm_roi_df,
  aes(
    x = roi,
    y = emmean,
    color = lexicality_condition,
    group = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = asymp.LCL, ymax = asymp.UCL),
    width = 0.12
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: Baseline passive-target predictability by ROI",
    subtitle = "Estimated marginal means from token_logprob ~ roi * lexicality_condition + (1 | item_id)",
    x = "Passive ROI",
    y = "Estimated token log-probability\n(higher / less negative = more predictable)",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1)
  )
```

```{r}
emm_roi_baseline <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_passive_roi_baseline,
  ~ roi | lexicality_condition
)

pairs(
  emm_roi_baseline,
  by = "lexicality_condition",
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

```{r}
emm_roi_by_lex <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_passive_roi_baseline,
  ~ roi | lexicality_condition
)

roi_levels <- levels(emm_roi_by_lex)$roi

aux_part_w <- setNames(rep(0, length(roi_levels)), roi_levels)
aux_part_w["aux"] <- 1
aux_part_w["participle"] <- -1

aux_part_gap <- contrast(
  emm_roi_by_lex,
  method = list("aux - participle" = aux_part_w),
  adjust = "none"
)

aux_part_gap

pairs(
  aux_part_gap,
  by = NULL,
  adjust = "none"
)
```

Once lexical semantics is removed or weakened, the participial region behaves more like a formal/morphological continuation of the passive frame. The model no longer has to predict a specific meaningful lexical verb to the same extent.

### Summary

- All models show positive no-prime passive bias.
- Jabberwocky reduces passive bias but does not eliminate it.
- 8B_instruct shows the strongest passive bias among the valid models.
- No simple monotonic size effect appears among base models.
- Instruction tuning increases passive bias strongly for 8B but not for 3B.
- Passive morphology increases next-token predictability. In core, the lexical-nature of the verb is way less predictable than its grammatical form: indeed, after parsing the entire form and seeing participle morpheme, the by-phrase becomes extremely predictable.

## RQ2: Baseline-Centered Priming Shift

RQ2.1: Relative to each item’s no-prime baseline, do active and passive primes shift the model’s active/passive preference in the expected direction?

H2.1: Passive primes will produce positive shift_from_no_prime, indicating movement toward the passive target. Active primes will produce negative shift_from_no_prime, indicating movement toward the active target.

RQ2.2: Are active and passive shifts symmetric in magnitude?

H2.2: Because Experiment 1B showed a strong no-prime passive bias, active primes may produce larger absolute movement from baseline than passive primes. This would suggest that active primes work partly by counteracting an already passive-biased scoring state.

RQ2.3: Does the baseline-centered shift differ by lexicality and model condition?

H2.3: Directional shifts should survive in jabberwocky but considering the lexical semantics contribution proven in preivious studies, shifts may be reduced in jabberwocky.

DV: shift_from_no_prime

```{r rq2-baseline-centered-shift}
exp1b_shifts <- baseline_shifts %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive"),
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(shift_from_no_prime)) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp1b_shift <- lmerTest::lmer(
  shift_from_no_prime ~ prime_type * lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_shifts,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp1b_shift, test = "F")
```

```{r rq2-estimated-marginal-means}
summary(m_exp1b_shift)
```

Baseline-centered priming shifts are not uniform. They depend on prime type, lexicality, and model condition, and are also modulated by passive-active token-count differences.

- Intercept: In CORE for 3B_base, an active prime shifts the model away from the passive target and toward the active target relative to no-prime baseline.
- passive priming does not boost passive preference above no-prime baseline.
- Items with larger passive-active token-count differences tend to show more active-oriented, or less passive-oriented, shifts from baseline.

That is interesting because in RQ1 token-count difference helped explain passive baseline bias, but in RQ2 it predicts less additional passive movement from baseline. This fits the idea that once the baseline is already passive-biased, there may be limited room for further passive increase.

### H2.1: Direction of active and passive shifts

Is this prime condition different from the no-prime baseline? Does priming move the model away from baseline?

```{r}
emm_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_shift,
  ~ prime_type | lexicality_condition * model_condition
)
test(emm_shift, null = 0)
```

- active primes are negative in every condition -\> active primes move the model towards active targets relative to baseline.
- passive primes are positive only for gpt-2-large in the jabber condition and they are near zero in the same condition for llama-8B -\> H2.1 for passive not supported.

Passive primes do not usually boost passive preference above the no-prime baseline. Instead, they produce a less active-oriented shift than active primes.

This is theoretically important. It means that because the no-prime baseline is already strongly passive-biased, passive primes often do not increase passive preference further. They mainly prevent the active-oriented movement produced by active primes.

```{r}
emm_shift_df <- as.data.frame(emm_shift)

ggplot(
  emm_shift_df,
  aes(x = prime_type, y = emmean, color = lexicality_condition, group = lexicality_condition)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.3), size = 2.8) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = asymp.LCL, ymax = asymp.UCL),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ model_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1: Baseline-centered priming shifts",
    x = "Prime type",
    y = "Shift from no-prime baseline",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

Value Meaning above 0 = shift toward passive below 0 = shift toward active 0 no change from no-prime baseline

```{r}
pairs(
  emm_shift,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

```{r}
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(
  pairs(
    emm_shift,
    by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_condition"),
    adjust = "holm"
  )
)

ggplot(
  contrast_df,
  aes(x = model_condition, y = estimate, color = lexicality_condition)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.35), size = 2.8) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * SE, ymax = estimate + 1.96 * SE),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: Active vs passive prime contrast",
    subtitle = "Negative values = passive primes are more passive-oriented than active primes",
    x = "Model condition",
    y = "Active shift − passive shift",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1))
```

Value Meaning 0 = active and passive primes shift equally negative = passive primes are more passive-oriented than active primes positive = active primes are more passive-oriented than passive primes

Active primes reliably shift the model toward active targets. Passive primes do not generally increase passive preference above baseline, but they do reliably produce more passive-oriented shifts than active primes.

Directional shifts survive in jabberwocky (H2.3a supported) but the contrast is amplified (H2.3b not supported).

### H2.2: Is the active-prime movement larger than the passive-prime movement, regardless of direction?

```{r}
exp1b_shifts_abs <- exp1b_shifts %>%
  filter(prime_type %in% c("active", "passive")) %>%
  mutate(abs_shift_from_no_prime = abs(shift_from_no_prime))

m_exp1b_abs_shift <- lmerTest::lmer(
  abs_shift_from_no_prime ~ prime_type * lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_shifts_abs,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_exp1b_abs_shift)
```

```{r}
emm_abs_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_abs_shift,
  ~ prime_type | lexicality_condition * model_condition
)

pairs(
  emm_abs_shift,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

The no-prime baseline is already passive-biased. Therefore, passive primes often have little room to increase passive preference further, whereas active primes can produce a stronger counter-shift away from that passive baseline. Passive primes are more passive-oriented than active primes, but active primes often create the larger baseline-relative movement.

```{r}
emm_abs_df <- as.data.frame(emm_abs_shift)

ggplot(
  emm_abs_df,
  aes(x = prime_type, y = emmean, color = lexicality_condition, group = lexicality_condition)
) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.3), size = 2.8) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = asymp.LCL, ymax = asymp.UCL),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ model_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: Absolute priming shifts from baseline",
    x = "Prime type",
    y = "Absolute shift from no-prime baseline",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

Higher point Meaning active higher than passive = active prime moves farther from baseline passive higher than active = passive prime moves farther from baseline

### H2.3: Lexicality and model condition

Directional shifts should survive in jabberwocky, but may be reduced if lexical semantics contributes.

Directional shifts survive in jabberwocky (H2.3a supported) but the contrast is amplified (H2.3b not supported).

### EXP2.1: ROI shift

#### Targets tokens predictability after congruent prime vs baseline

##### Passive targets

```{r}
exp1b_passive_tokens <- token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    candidate_voice == "passive",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active", "passive"),
    roi %in% passive_roi_order
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = passive_roi_order)
  ) %>%
  group_by(
    item_id,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_roi_logprob = mean(token_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

plot_passive_prime_collapsed <- exp1b_passive_tokens %>%
  filter(prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "passive")) %>%
  group_by(lexicality_condition, prime_condition, roi) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_logprob = mean(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(
  plot_passive_prime_collapsed,
  aes(x = roi, y = mean_logprob, color = prime_condition, group = prime_condition)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = mean_logprob - 1.96 * se,
        ymax = mean_logprob + 1.96 * se),
    width = 0.12
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicality_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: passive target under no-prime vs passive-prime",
    subtitle = "Collapsed across models; higher values = more predictable token",
    x = "Passive ROI",
    y = "Mean token log-probability",
    color = "Condition"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(
    patient_np = "Patient",
    aux = "Aux",
    participle = "Participle",
    by = "by",
    agent_det = "Agent det",
    agent_np = "Agent"
  )) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

passive targets already have high no-prime predictability in CORE, so passive primes have little room to raise them further. when semantic content is weakened, the prime’s structural information becomes more visible.

##### Active targets

```{r}
active_roi_order <- c(
  "initial_det",
  "agent_np",
  "verb",
  "patient_det",
  "patient_np",
  "punctuation"
)

exp1b_active_tokens <- token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    candidate_voice == "active",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active"),
    roi %in% active_roi_order
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = active_roi_order),
    lexicality_condition = factor(
      lexicality_condition,
      levels = c("core", "jabberwocky")
    ),
    prime_condition = factor(
      prime_condition,
      levels = c("no_prime", "active")
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(
    item_id,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_roi_logprob = mean(token_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

plot_active_prime_collapsed <- exp1b_active_tokens %>%
  group_by(lexicality_condition, prime_condition, roi) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_logprob = mean(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(
  plot_active_prime_collapsed,
  aes(
    x = roi,
    y = mean_logprob,
    color = prime_condition,
    group = prime_condition
  )
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = mean_logprob - 1.96 * se,
      ymax = mean_logprob + 1.96 * se
    ),
    width = 0.12
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicality_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: active target under no-prime vs active-prime",
    subtitle = "Collapsed across models; higher values = more predictable token",
    x = "Active ROI",
    y = "Mean token log-probability",
    color = "Condition"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(
    initial_det = "Init det",
    agent_np = "Agent",
    verb = "Verb",
    patient_det = "Patient det",
    patient_np = "Patient",
    punctuation = "."
  )) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

Active targets are less overtly marked, but active priming still works

This is theoretically interesting. Actives do not have the same overt morphosyntactic skeleton as passives — no auxiliary, no participial morphology, no by-phrase.

Yet in jabberwocky, active priming strongly boosts active target predictability.

That means the active-prime effect cannot be only about passive-specific function words. It likely reflects something like:

agent-first mapping → verb → patient continuation

So the active structural signal is present, but it is less encoded in overt morphology and more dependent on role/order expectations.

#### Targets tokens predictability after congruent prime vs baseline x model

##### Passive Targets

```{r}
roi_order <- c("patient_np", "aux", "participle", "by", "agent_det", "agent_np")

exp1b_passive_tokens <- token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    candidate_voice == "passive",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active", "passive"),
    roi %in% roi_order
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = roi_order)
  ) %>%
  group_by(
    item_id,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_roi_logprob = mean(token_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

plot_passive_prime <- exp1b_passive_tokens %>%
  filter(prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "passive")) %>%
  group_by(model_condition, lexicality_condition, prime_condition, roi) %>%
  summarise(
    emmean = mean(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(
  plot_passive_prime,
  aes(x = roi, y = emmean, color = prime_condition, group = prime_condition)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = emmean - 1.96 * se, ymax = emmean + 1.96 * se),
    width = 0.12
  ) +
  facet_grid(lexicality_condition ~ model_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: passive target under no-prime vs passive-prime",
    subtitle = "Higher / less negative values = more predictable token",
    x = "Passive ROI",
    y = "Mean token log-probability",
    color = "Condition"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1))
```

##### Active targets

```{r}
active_roi_order <- c(
  "initial_det",
  "agent_np",
  "verb",
  "patient_det",
  "patient_np",
  "punctuation"
)

plot_active_prime_by_model <- token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    candidate_voice == "active",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active"),
    roi %in% active_roi_order
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = active_roi_order),
    lexicality_condition = factor(
      lexicality_condition,
      levels = c("core", "jabberwocky")
    ),
    prime_condition = factor(
      prime_condition,
      levels = c("no_prime", "active")
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(
    item_id,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_roi_logprob = mean(token_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  group_by(
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_logprob = mean(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(mean_roi_logprob, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(
  plot_active_prime_by_model,
  aes(
    x = roi,
    y = mean_logprob,
    color = prime_condition,
    group = prime_condition
  )
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = mean_logprob - 1.96 * se,
      ymax = mean_logprob + 1.96 * se
    ),
    width = 0.12
  ) +
  facet_grid(lexicality_condition ~ model_condition) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(
    initial_det = "Init det",
    agent_np = "Agent",
    verb = "Verb",
    patient_det = "Patient det",
    patient_np = "Patient",
    punctuation = "."
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 1B: active target under no-prime vs active-prime",
    subtitle = "Higher / less negative values = more predictable token",
    x = "Active ROI",
    y = "Mean token log-probability",
    color = "Condition"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
  )
```

### EXPL2.2: IFE

Premise: previous results show how passive structures, though less frequent as a constructional choice, seem highly predictable once its morphosyntactic frame is underway. It decomposes into different predictive events. There seems to be no IFE but there seems to be a residual stream effect.

```{r}
token_roi <- readr::read_csv(
  file.path(input_dir, "token_roi_summary.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  canonicalize_common()
```

Passive target after passive prime

```{r}
passive_roi_order <- c(
  "patient_np",
  "aux",
  "participle",
  "by",
  "agent_det",
  "agent_np"
)

passive_roi <- token_roi %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    candidate_voice == "passive",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "passive"),
    roi %in% passive_roi_order
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = passive_roi_order)
  ) %>%
  select(
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi,
    roi_surprisal_mean
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = prime_condition,
    values_from = roi_surprisal_mean
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    passive_prime_facilitation = no_prime - passive
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(passive_prime_facilitation))

m_passive_roi_facilitation <- lm(
  passive_prime_facilitation ~ roi * lexicality_condition,
  data = passive_roi,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_passive_roi_facilitation)
```

```{r}
emm_passive_roi_facilitation <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_passive_roi_facilitation,
  ~ roi | lexicality_condition
)

test(emm_passive_roi_facilitation, null = 0)
```

Value Meaning positive passive prime reduced surprisal / increased predictability negative passive prime increased surprisal / reduced predictability zero no passive-prime effect relative to baseline

Passive-prime facilitation is weak and uneven in CORE but strong and structurally localized in jabberwocky.

Active target after active prime

```{r}
active_roi_order <- c(
  "initial_det",
  "agent_np",
  "verb",
  "patient_det",
  "patient_np",
  "punctuation"
)

active_roi <- token_roi %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    candidate_voice == "active",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active"),
    roi %in% active_roi_order
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    roi = factor(roi, levels = active_roi_order)
  ) %>%
  select(
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_condition,
    roi,
    roi_surprisal_mean
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = prime_condition,
    values_from = roi_surprisal_mean
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    active_prime_facilitation = no_prime - active
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(active_prime_facilitation))

m_active_roi_facilitation <- lm(
  active_prime_facilitation ~ roi * lexicality_condition,
  data = active_roi,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_active_roi_facilitation)
```

```{r}
emm_active_roi_facilitation <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_active_roi_facilitation,
  ~ roi | lexicality_condition
)

test(emm_active_roi_facilitation, null = 0)
```

Estimate Meaning positive = active prime reduced surprisal / increased predictability negative = active prime increased surprisal / reduced predictability zero = no active-prime effect

Does surprisal while processing the prime predict the size of the later target shift?

```{r}
item_level <- item_level %>%
  mutate(
    active_surprisal_mean = -active_choice_logprob,
    passive_surprisal_mean = -passive_choice_logprob,
    active_surprisal_sum = -active_choice_logprob_sum,
    passive_surprisal_sum = -passive_choice_logprob_sum
  )

prime_token_level <- readr::read_csv(
  file.path(input_dir, "token_level_probabilities.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  canonicalize_common()

prime_token_level <- prime_token_level %>%
  mutate(
    prime_type = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_condition %in% c("active", "active_prime") ~ "active",
      prime_condition %in% c("passive", "passive_prime") ~ "passive",
      TRUE ~ as.character(prime_condition)
    )
  )

```

```{r}
prime_surprisal_item <- prime_token_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive")
  ) %>%
  group_by(
    experiment,
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    prime_type
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    prime_surprisal_mean = mean(token_surprisal, na.rm = TRUE),
    prime_surprisal_sum = sum(token_surprisal, na.rm = TRUE),
    prime_token_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

```

```{r}
exp1b_ife <- baseline_shifts %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_1b",
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(shift_from_no_prime)
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    prime_surprisal_item,
    by = c(
      "experiment",
      "item_id",
      "item_index",
      "model_condition",
      "lexicality_condition",
      "prime_type"
    )
  )

```

```{r}
exp1b_ife <- exp1b_ife %>%
  mutate(
    congruent_shift = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_type == "passive" ~ shift_from_no_prime,
      prime_type == "active"  ~ -shift_from_no_prime,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(model_condition, lexicality_condition, prime_type) %>%
  mutate(
    prime_surprisal_z = as.numeric(scale(prime_surprisal_mean))
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(congruent_shift),
    !is.na(prime_surprisal_z)
  )
```

```{r}
m_exp1b_ife <- lm(
  congruent_shift ~ prime_surprisal_z * prime_type * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z,
  data = exp1b_ife,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)
drop1(m_exp1b_ife, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp1b_ife)
```

Slope direction

```{r}
ife_slopes <- emmeans::emtrends(
  m_exp1b_ife,
  ~ prime_type * lexicality_condition,
  var = "prime_surprisal_z"
)

ife_slopes
test(ife_slopes, null = 0)
```

IFE predicts positive slopes. -\> all slopes are negative.

```{r}
pairs(
  ife_slopes,
  by = "lexicality_condition",
  adjust = "holm"
)

pairs(
  ife_slopes,
  by = "prime_type",
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

### Conclusions

- Baseline passive bias: passives are already preferred before priming.
- ROI baseline profile: passive function-word regions are highly predictable.
- Passive-prime CORE ceiling: passive prime adds little where baseline is already high.
- Jabberwocky amplification: structural priming becomes clearer when lexical semantics is suppressed.
- Active-prime counter-shift: active primes often move more from baseline because they counteract passive bias.
- No IFE: Zhou et al. 2025 found IFE with datives but the effect was tight to verb-framing bias, semantically mediated. Jumelet et al. 2024 focus on datives and argue that LLM priming asymmetries are predictable from factors like surprisal and verb preference.

But Sinclair et al. did not find stronger priming for the semantically implausible-prime corpus. They found the opposite. In their §7.2 semantic implausibility experiment, they built primes that were grammatical but nonsensical, e.g. “the iron threw the hero to the chocolate,” while preserving the no-overlap/no-semantic-similarity constraints from the core corpus. Their result was that PE became negative for active and PO constructions, while passive and DO PE decreased relative to core but remained positive. They conclude that structural encoding is not fully independent from semantic plausibility.

Sinclair et al. 2022 already show that making the prime semantically implausible does not simply make the structural signal stronger. Semantic disruption can weaken or distort priming.

Objection: Whole-target log-probability PE is not a clean measure of structural priming. In active/passive alternations, it mixes baseline constructional bias, local morphosyntactic cue predictability, lexical-semantic predictability, and possible prime-induced updating. Therefore, sentence-level PE alone cannot distinguish priming from incremental surface form predictability.

Interpretation: This priming scoring task seems more similar to Paolazzi et al 2019 results in which they found that passive sentences are read faster than actives but there was a dissociation with comprehension showing the inverse effect. Humans process language at multiple levels simultaneously, could it be that the LLM only performs one task like brainlessly processing strings

# EXPERIMENT 2

## RQ1: Baseline Active/Passive Bias

RQ1.1: Before priming, do models prefer passive or active targets in a generation task? and does this vary by lexicality, and model type/size?

H1.1: In contrast to Exp1B, where passive targets received higher scored probability, we predict that in a free generation task models will preferentially generate active sentences at baseline. This is because generation requires choosing an event-to-syntax mapping, and the canonical agent-first active frame should dominate when the model is not forced to compare active and passive target strings.

```{r}
exp2_baseline <- exp2_generation %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_2",
    prime_condition == "no_prime",
    lexicality_condition %in% c("core", "mixed"),
    generation_voice %in% c("active", "passive")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    generated_passive = as.integer(generation_voice == "passive")
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

exp2_baseline_counts <- exp2_baseline %>%
  count(generation_voice) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = generation_voice,
    values_from = n,
    values_fill = 0
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    total = active + passive,
    passive_rate = passive / total,
    active_rate = active / total
  )

exp2_baseline_counts

binom.test(
  x = exp2_baseline_counts$passive,
  n = exp2_baseline_counts$total,
  p = 0.5,
  alternative = "less"
)
```

In free generation, models overwhelmingly prefer the active frame at baseline.

```{r}

m_exp2_baseline <- lme4::glmer(
  generated_passive ~ lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp2_baseline,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)
drop1(m_exp2_baseline, test = "Chisq")
```

```{r}
m_exp2_baseline_additive <- lme4::glmer(
  generated_passive ~ lexicality_condition + model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp2_baseline,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp2_baseline_additive, test = "Chisq")
```

```{r}
m_exp2_baseline_simple <- lme4::glmer(
  generated_passive ~ model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp2_baseline,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_exp2_baseline_simple)
```

Do model conditions differ in how often they generate passives at baseline? Yes. But all models are active-biased.

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

exp2_baseline_plot_df <- exp2_baseline %>%
  count(model_condition, generation_voice) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = generation_voice,
    values_from = n,
    values_fill = 0
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    total = active + passive,
    passive_rate = passive / total,
    active_rate = active / total,

    # Exact binomial confidence intervals
    ci_low = ifelse(
      passive == 0,
      0,
      qbeta(0.025, passive, active + 1)
    ),
    ci_high = ifelse(
      passive == total,
      1,
      qbeta(0.975, passive + 1, active)
    ),

    model_condition = factor(
      model_condition,
      levels = c(
        "774M_base",
        "3B_base",
        "3B_instruct",
        "8B_base",
        "8B_instruct",
        "9B_base"
      )
    )
  )

ggplot(
  exp2_baseline_plot_df,
  aes(x = model_condition, y = passive_rate)
) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0.5,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = paste0(
        round(passive_rate * 100, 2),
        "%\n",
        passive, "/", total
      )
    ),
    vjust = -0.8,
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(exp2_baseline_plot_df$ci_high, na.rm = TRUE) + 0.05)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 2: baseline passive generation by model",
    subtitle = "No-prime condition; dashed line = 50% passive generation",
    x = "Model condition",
    y = "Observed passive-generation rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

## RQ2: Priming Effect

RQ2.2: Do active and passive primes affect the probability that the model generates an active or passive sentence? 
H2.1: Active primes should increase active generation, whereas passive primes should increase passive generation. Passive priming may be more visible in Exp2 than in Exp1B because generation requires mapping event roles onto syntax. If passive difficulty lies less in surface-form prediction and more in event-role mapping, then passive primes may provide a stronger cue in this task than in fixed-target scoring. We treat IFE as an exploratory mechanism: if prime surprisal predicts generation shift, this would support an error-driven interpretation.

```{r}
exp2_prime <- exp2_generation %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_2",
    lexicality_condition %in% c("core", "mixed"),
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "filler", "active", "passive"),
    generation_voice %in% c("active", "passive")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    generated_passive = as.integer(generation_voice == "passive"),
    lexicality_condition = factor(
      lexicality_condition,
      levels = c("core", "mixed")
    ),
    prime_condition = factor(
      prime_condition,
      levels = c("no_prime", "filler", "active", "passive")
    )
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

exp2_prime %>%
  count(lexicality_condition, prime_condition, generation_voice) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = generation_voice,
    values_from = n,
    values_fill = 0
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    total = active + passive,
    passive_rate = passive / total
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

```{r}
m_exp2_prime <- lme4::glmer(
  generated_passive ~ prime_condition * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp2_prime,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)


drop1(m_exp2_prime, test = "Chisq")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp2_prime)
```

```{r}
emm_exp2_prime <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp2_prime,
  ~ prime_condition | lexicality_condition,
  type = "response"
)

emm_exp2_prime
```

```{r}
emm_exp2_prime_link <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp2_prime,
  ~ prime_condition | lexicality_condition,
  type = "link"
)

exp2_prime_contrasts <- contrast(
  emm_exp2_prime_link,
  method = list(
    "filler - no_prime"  = c(-1,  1,  0,  0),
    "active - no_prime"  = c(-1,  0,  1,  0),
    "passive - no_prime" = c(-1,  0,  0,  1),
    "passive - active"   = c( 0,  0, -1,  1)
  ),
  by = "lexicality_condition",
  adjust = "holm"
)

exp2_prime_contrasts
```

```{r}
exp2_prime_plot_df <- as.data.frame(emm_exp2_prime)

ggplot(
  exp2_prime_plot_df,
  aes(
    x = prime_condition,
    y = prob,
    color = lexicality_condition,
    group = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.35), size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = asymp.LCL, ymax = asymp.UCL),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 2: passive generation by prime condition",
    subtitle = "Estimated probabilities from binomial GLMM",
    x = "Prime condition",
    y = "Estimated probability of passive generation",
    color = "Condition"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

```{r}
emm_exp2_prime_prob <- as.data.frame(
  emmeans::emmeans(
    m_exp2_prime,
    ~ prime_condition | lexicality_condition,
    type = "response"
  )
)

exp2_prob_shifts <- emm_exp2_prime_prob %>%
  select(lexicality_condition, prime_condition, prob) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = prime_condition,
    values_from = prob
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    filler_shift = filler - no_prime,
    active_shift = active - no_prime,
    passive_shift = passive - no_prime
  )

exp2_prob_shifts
```

```{r}
emm_exp2_prime_by_model_link <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp2_prime,
  ~ prime_condition | lexicality_condition * model_condition,
  type = "link"
)

exp2_shift_by_model <- contrast(
  emm_exp2_prime_by_model_link,
  method = list(
    "active - no_prime"  = c(-1,  0,  1,  0),
    "passive - no_prime" = c(-1,  0,  0,  1)
  ),
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(
    contrast = factor(
      contrast,
      levels = c(
        "active - no_prime",
        "passive - no_prime"
      )
    )
  )

ggplot(
  exp2_shift_by_model,
  aes(x = model_condition, y = estimate, color = contrast)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.55),
    size = 2.7
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * SE,
        ymax = estimate + 1.96 * SE),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.55),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicality_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 2: baseline-centered generation shifts by model",
    subtitle = "Log-odds contrasts relative to no-prime baseline",
    x = "Model condition",
    y = "Shift in log-odds of passive generation",
    color = "Prime contrast"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
emm_exp2_prime_by_model_prob <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp2_prime,
  ~ prime_condition | lexicality_condition * model_condition,
  type = "response"
) %>%
  as.data.frame()

exp2_prob_shift_by_model <- emm_exp2_prime_by_model_prob %>%
  select(lexicality_condition, model_condition, prime_condition, prob) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = prime_condition,
    values_from = prob
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    active_shift = active - no_prime,
    passive_shift = passive - no_prime
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(active_shift, passive_shift),
    names_to = "shift_type",
    values_to = "probability_shift"
  )

ggplot(
  exp2_prob_shift_by_model,
  aes(x = model_condition, y = probability_shift, color = shift_type)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.55),
    size = 2.7
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicality_condition) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 2: probability shifts from no-prime baseline",
    subtitle = "Response-scale change in passive-generation probability",
    x = "Model condition",
    y = "Change in probability of passive generation",
    color = "Shift type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

#### EXPL2: IFE

H2.4: In Experiment 1B, the model does not need to understand the event and then choose a structure. It only scores two fixed target strings. In that setting, passives get a boost because their morphosyntactic spine is highly predictable.

But in a production task, the model has to do something closer to:

1.  parse the prime
2.  map roles: who did what to whom
3.  process a new target event
4.  choose how to describe that event

In that setting, the passive prime may create a stronger prediction error because the event roles are mapped onto syntax in a less canonical way:

patient/theme → subject agent → by-phrase

Then, if the model updates from that unexpected mapping, it may be more likely to reuse the passive structure when describing the next event.

That is exactly the kind of situation where an inverse frequency / inverse preference effect would be expected.

```{r}
exp2_prime_surprisal <- readr::read_csv(
  file.path(input_dir, "exp2_prime_surprisal_item_level.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  canonicalize_common()

names(exp2_prime_surprisal)
```

```{r}
exp2_prime_surprisal_clean <- exp2_prime_surprisal %>%
  mutate(
    exp2_lexicality = dplyr::case_when(
      lexicality_condition == "core" ~ "core",
      lexicality_condition %in% c("mixed", "core_targets_jabberwocky_primes") ~ "mixed",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    prime_type = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_condition %in% c("active", "active_prime") ~ "active",
      prime_condition %in% c("passive", "passive_prime") ~ "passive",
      TRUE ~ as.character(prime_condition)
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_2",
    exp2_lexicality %in% c("core", "mixed"),
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive")
  ) %>%
  select(
    experiment,
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    exp2_lexicality,
    prime_type,
    prime_surprisal_mean,
    prime_surprisal_sum
  )

exp2_ife <- exp2_generation %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_2",
    lexicality_condition %in% c("core", "mixed", "core_targets_jabberwocky_primes"),
    prime_condition %in% c("active", "passive"),
    generation_voice %in% c("active", "passive")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    exp2_lexicality = dplyr::case_when(
      lexicality_condition == "core" ~ "core",
      lexicality_condition %in% c("mixed", "core_targets_jabberwocky_primes") ~ "mixed",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    prime_type = as.character(prime_condition),
    congruent_generation = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_type == "active"  & generation_voice == "active"  ~ 1,
      prime_type == "passive" & generation_voice == "passive" ~ 1,
      prime_type == "active"  & generation_voice == "passive" ~ 0,
      prime_type == "passive" & generation_voice == "active"  ~ 0,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    exp2_prime_surprisal_clean,
    by = c(
      "experiment",
      "item_id",
      "item_index",
      "model_condition",
      "exp2_lexicality",
      "prime_type"
    )
  )
exp2_ife <- exp2_ife %>%
  group_by(model_condition, exp2_lexicality, prime_type) %>%
  mutate(
    prime_surprisal_z = as.numeric(scale(prime_surprisal_mean))
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(congruent_generation),
    !is.na(prime_surprisal_z)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    exp2_lexicality = factor(exp2_lexicality, levels = c("core", "mixed")),
    prime_type = factor(prime_type, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  )
```

```{r}
m_exp2_ife <- lme4::glmer(
  congruent_generation ~ prime_surprisal_z * prime_type * exp2_lexicality +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp2_ife,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp2_ife, test = "Chisq")
```

```{r}
m_exp2_ife2 <- lme4::glmer(
  congruent_generation ~ prime_surprisal_z + prime_type + exp2_lexicality +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp2_ife,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp2_ife2, test = "Chisq")
```

NO IFE!!!! Exp2 shows strong structural priming in generation, but sentence-level prime surprisal does not predict congruent generation. This argues against a simple IFE account. However, because IFE effects in prior LLM work are closely tied to lexical/verb-specific and structural preferences, a more targeted semantic or event-role surprisal measure may be needed to test whether error-driven updating operates at the event-mapping level.

Summary: 
\* baseline generation is strongly active-bias 
\* passive primes increase passive generation 
\* active prime increase active generation but does not move significantly the already active baseline 
\* jabber passive primes boost priming even more than core -\> structural signal becomes clearer when lexical semantics is attenuated 
\* active priming is stronger in core than jabber but baseline is already at ceiling

Exp2 is compatible with the idea that event-to-syntax mapping reveals stronger passive priming

RQ2.3: Do Exp1B scoring preferences and Exp2 generation choices align? 
H2.3: We predict a dissociation: scored log-probability preference and free construction choice are not the same process. Exp1B may show passive preference because passive strings contain predictable morphosyntactic cues, whereas Exp2 may show active preference because generation favors canonical agent-first event expression. -\> supported by results

# Experiment 3: event-to-syntax mapping with fix-target scoring

RQ1: Does the Exp1B passive scoring bias persist when the model is explicitly given event-role information, and does role mapping make priming effects stronger? H1: We predict the same qualitative baseline pattern as Exp1B: models will show positive passive log-odds, indicating a passive scoring preference. This prediction follows from Exp1B: passive targets contain highly predictable morphosyntactic cues, so even when event roles are explicit, the passive target may still receive higher mean-token probability than the active target.

Primary DV:

``` text
logodds_passive = logP(passive target) - logP(active target) -> for this exact same event, does the model assign more probability to the passive wording or the active wording?
```

```{r}
# EXPERIMENT 3 — RQ1: Baseline active/passive bias

exp3_baseline <- item_level %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_3",
    task_type == "event_to_syntax_mapping",
    prime_condition == "no_prime"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(logodds_passive)) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp3_baseline <- lmerTest::lmer(
  logodds_passive ~ lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp3_baseline,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)


drop1(m_exp3_baseline, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp3_baseline)
```

- In CORE, most models are close to zero, with only modest passive bias.
- In jabberwocky, most models show a clearer passive preference, except 9B_base, which reverses strongly. when lexical-semantic information is reduced, the structural/formal passive signal becomes more visible.
- larger passive-active token-count differences are associated with higher passive log-odds. Event roles mapping engagement attenuates passive bias.

```{r}
emm_exp3_baseline <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp3_baseline,
  ~ lexicality_condition * model_condition
)

test(
  emm_exp3_baseline,
  null = 0
)
```

```{r}
pairs(
  emmeans::emmeans(
    m_exp3_baseline,
    ~ lexicality_condition | model_condition
  ),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

```{r}
emm_exp3_baseline_df <- as.data.frame(emm_exp3_baseline)

ggplot(
  emm_exp3_baseline_df,
  aes(
    x = model_condition,
    y = emmean,
    color = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = asymp.LCL,
      ymax = asymp.UCL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 3: no-prime active/passive baseline bias",
    subtitle = "Positive values indicate passive target preference",
    x = "Model condition",
    y = "Estimated passive log-odds",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

- core: most models are close to zero
- jabberwocky: clearer passive preference than in core exept for gemma which has active preference at baseline This seems to suggest that the big passive bias in experiment 1 was tight to the scoring setup and passive surface form predictability. Exp3 makes event roles explicit. If the model is using those roles to map event participants onto syntax, then the canonical active mapping becomes more competitive.

That reduces the passive advantage, especially in CORE.

But in jabberwocky, lexical-semantic content is reduced, so formal structural cues become more important again. That may explain why most jabberwocky conditions show stronger passive bias than CORE.

RQ2: Do active and passive primes shift event-to-syntax target scoring away from each item’s no-prime baseline? H2: Active primes should shift the model toward active targets, and passive primes should shift the model toward passive targets. However, because Exp3 requires mapping explicit thematic roles onto syntactic form, we predict that passive primes may produce a stronger positive baseline-centered shift than in Exp1B.

```{r}
exp3_shifts <- baseline_shifts %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_3",
    task_type == "event_to_syntax_mapping",
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive")
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(shift_from_no_prime)) %>%
  mutate(
    prime_type = factor(prime_type, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp3_shift <- lmerTest::lmer(
  shift_from_no_prime ~ prime_type * lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp3_shifts,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)


drop1(m_exp3_shift, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp3_shift)
```

- active primes active (positive shift)
- passive primes passive (negative shift)
- jabber strenghtens passive prime shift for some models
- token length effect is small but significant

```{r}
emm_exp3_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp3_shift,
  ~ prime_type | lexicality_condition * model_condition
)

test(
  emm_exp3_shift,
  null = 0
)
```

- clear bidirectional priming

```{r}
pairs(
  emm_exp3_shift,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

In every lexicality × model condition, passive primes produce a more passive-oriented shift than active primes.

RQ3: Is the baseline-centered priming effect stronger in Exp3 than Exp1B?

H3: If Exp1B primarily reflects surface-string predictability, but Exp3 additionally recruits event-role mapping, then priming should be more visible in Exp3, especially for passive primes. A stronger passive-prime shift in Exp3 would suggest that passive priming emerges more clearly when the model must align syntax with event role

```{r}
exp3_shifts_abs <- exp3_shifts %>%
  mutate(
    abs_shift_from_no_prime = abs(shift_from_no_prime)
  )

m_exp3_abs_shift <- lmerTest::lmer(
  abs_shift_from_no_prime ~ prime_type * lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp3_shifts_abs,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)


drop1(m_exp3_abs_shift, test = "F")
```

```{r}
m_exp3_abs_shift2 <- lmerTest::lmer(
  abs_shift_from_no_prime ~ prime_type * lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp3_shifts_abs,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp3_abs_shift2, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp3_abs_shift2)
```

- the relative magnitude of active vs passive shifts depends strongly on lexicality and model condition.
- In CORE, passive primes generally produce larger absolute shifts than active primes.
- In jabberwocky, the pattern is model-dependent

the baseline passive bias is much weaker, and passive primes produce clear positive shifts. Therefore, passive primes often produce larger absolute movement, especially in CORE.

```{r}
emm_exp3_abs_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp3_abs_shift,
  ~ prime_type | lexicality_condition * model_condition
)

pairs(
  emm_exp3_abs_shift,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "model_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

In that task, lexical semantics may actually help. Real verbs and real nouns provide anchors for event interpretation. When you replace them with jabberwocky material, you remove semantic noise, but you also remove useful cues for event-role mapping.

So the effect of jabberwocky changes by task:

Task What jabberwocky does Exp1B fixed-target scoring reduces semantic noise and exposes form predictability Exp3 event-to-syntax mapping removes semantic anchors needed for role-to-syntax mapping

```{r}
emm_exp3_shift_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(emm_exp3_shift, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

ggplot(
  emm_exp3_shift_df,
  aes(
    x = prime_type,
    y = emmean,
    color = lexicality_condition,
    group = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = asymp.LCL, ymax = asymp.UCL),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ model_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 3: baseline-centered priming shifts",
    subtitle = "Positive = shift toward passive; negative = shift toward active",
    x = "Prime type",
    y = "Shift from no-prime baseline",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

Passive primes produce larger absolute movement from baseline than active primes.

In CORE event-to-syntax mapping, passive primes produce both the expected direction and the larger movement from baseline. This suggests that explicit role-mapping makes passive priming more visible than in Exp1B.

```{r}
exp1b_exp3_shifts <- baseline_shifts %>%
  filter(
    experiment %in% c("experiment_1b", "experiment_3"),
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(shift_from_no_prime)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    experiment_label = dplyr::recode(
      experiment,
      experiment_1b = "Exp1B: fixed-target scoring",
      experiment_3  = "Exp3: event-to-syntax mapping"
    ),
    experiment_label = factor(
      experiment_label,
      levels = c(
        "Exp1B: fixed-target scoring",
        "Exp3: event-to-syntax mapping"
      )
    ),
    prime_type = factor(prime_type, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp1b_exp3_shift <- lmerTest::lmer(
  shift_from_no_prime ~ experiment_label * prime_type * lexicality_condition * model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_exp3_shifts,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp1b_exp3_shift, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp1b_exp3_shift)
```

Passive-prime shifts are more positive in Exp3 than in Exp1B.

```{r}
exp1b_exp3_shifts_simple <- baseline_shifts %>%
  filter(
    experiment %in% c("experiment_1b", "experiment_3"),
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(shift_from_no_prime)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    experiment_label = dplyr::recode(
      experiment,
      experiment_1b = "Exp1B_fixed_target",
      experiment_3  = "Exp3_event_to_syntax"
    ),
    experiment_label = factor(
      experiment_label,
      levels = c("Exp1B_fixed_target", "Exp3_event_to_syntax")
    ),
    prime_type = factor(prime_type, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp1b_exp3_shift_simple <- lmerTest::lmer(
  shift_from_no_prime ~ experiment_label * prime_type * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_exp3_shifts_simple,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_exp1b_exp3_shift_simple)
drop1(m_exp1b_exp3_shift_simple, test = "F")
```

```{r}
emm_exp1b_exp3_by_prime <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_exp3_shift_simple,
  ~ experiment_label | prime_type * lexicality_condition
)

contrast(
  emm_exp1b_exp3_by_prime,
  method = list("Exp3 - Exp1B" = c(-1, 1)),
  by = c("prime_type", "lexicality_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

active and passive priming shifted more in the expected direction in Exp3.

```{r}
exp1b_exp3_congruent <- exp1b_exp3_shifts_simple %>%
  mutate(
    congruent_shift = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_type == "passive" ~ shift_from_no_prime,
      prime_type == "active"  ~ -shift_from_no_prime,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  )

m_exp1b_exp3_congruent <- lmerTest::lmer(
  congruent_shift ~ experiment_label * prime_type * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    passive_minus_active_token_count_z +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_exp3_congruent,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp1b_exp3_congruent, test = "F")
```

```{r}
m_exp1b_exp3_congruent2 <- lmerTest::lmer(
  congruent_shift ~ experiment_label * prime_type * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_exp3_congruent,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp1b_exp3_congruent2, test = "F")
```

```{r}
m_exp1b_exp3_congruent2lm <- lm(
  congruent_shift ~ experiment_label * prime_type * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition,
  data = exp1b_exp3_congruent,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_exp1b_exp3_congruent2lm)
```

```{r}
emm_exp1b_exp3_congruent2lm <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_exp3_congruent2lm,
  ~ experiment_label | prime_type * lexicality_condition
)

contrast(
  emm_exp1b_exp3_congruent2lm,
  method = list("Exp3 - Exp1B" = c(-1, 1)),
  by = c("prime_type", "lexicality_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

Passive congruent shifts were boosted in Exp3. Active congruent shifts were smaller in Exp3 than in Exp1B.

```{r}
emm_exp1b_exp3_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_exp3_shift,
  ~ prime_type | experiment_label * lexicality_condition * model_condition
)

emm_exp1b_exp3_shift_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(emm_exp1b_exp3_shift, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

# Robust CI column handling
ci_low_col <- intersect(c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL"), names(emm_exp1b_exp3_shift_df))[1]
ci_high_col <- intersect(c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL"), names(emm_exp1b_exp3_shift_df))[1]
```
