```{r}
emm_exp1b_exp3_shift_df <- emm_exp1b_exp3_shift_df %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low = .data[[ci_low_col]],
    ci_high = .data[[ci_high_col]]
  )

ggplot(
  emm_exp1b_exp3_shift_df,
  aes(
    x = prime_type,
    y = emmean,
    color = experiment_label,
    group = experiment_label
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),
    size = 2.7
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_grid(lexicality_condition ~ model_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Baseline-centered priming shifts: Exp1B vs Exp3",
    subtitle = "Positive values = shift toward passive; negative values = shift toward active",
    x = "Prime type",
    y = "Shift from no-prime baseline",
    color = "Experiment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(emmeans)
library(lmerTest)

# Build congruent-shift dataframe
exp1b_exp3_congruent <- baseline_shifts %>%
  filter(
    experiment %in% c("experiment_1b", "experiment_3"),
    prime_type %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(shift_from_no_prime)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    experiment_label = dplyr::recode(
      experiment,
      experiment_1b = "Exp1B fixed-target",
      experiment_3  = "Exp3 event-to-syntax"
    ),
    experiment_label = factor(
      experiment_label,
      levels = c("Exp1B fixed-target", "Exp3 event-to-syntax")
    ),
    prime_type = factor(prime_type, levels = c("active", "passive")),

    # Put active and passive shifts on same congruent scale
    congruent_shift = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_type == "active"  ~ -shift_from_no_prime,
      prime_type == "passive" ~  shift_from_no_prime,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp1b_exp3_congruent <- lmerTest::lmer(
  congruent_shift ~ experiment_label * prime_type * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp1b_exp3_congruent,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp1b_exp3_congruent, test = "F")
```

```{r}
emm_congruent <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_exp3_congruent,
  ~ experiment_label | prime_type * lexicality_condition
)

emm_congruent <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp1b_exp3_congruent,
  ~ experiment_label | prime_type * lexicality_condition
)

exp3_minus_exp1b <- contrast(
  emm_congruent,
  method = list("Exp3 - Exp1B" = c(-1, 1)),
  by = c("prime_type", "lexicality_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)

exp3_minus_exp1b_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(exp3_minus_exp1b, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

ci_low_col <- intersect(
  c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL", "LCL"),
  names(exp3_minus_exp1b_df)
)[1]

ci_high_col <- intersect(
  c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL", "UCL"),
  names(exp3_minus_exp1b_df)
)[1]

exp3_minus_exp1b_df <- exp3_minus_exp1b_df %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low = .data[[ci_low_col]],
    ci_high = .data[[ci_high_col]]
  )

ggplot(
  exp3_minus_exp1b_df,
  aes(
    x = prime_type,
    y = estimate,
    color = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Change in congruent priming from Exp1B to Exp3",
    subtitle = "Positive values = stronger congruent priming in Exp3",
    x = "Prime type",
    y = "Exp3 − Exp1B congruent shift",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

#### EXPL 3: IFE

```{r}
exp3_ife <- readr::read_csv(
  file.path(input_dir, "exp3_prime_surprisal_item_level.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  canonicalize_common()

names(exp3_ife)
```

```{r}
exp3_ife_clean <- exp3_ife %>%
  filter(
    experiment == "experiment_3",
    prime_condition %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(prime_surprisal_mean),
    !is.na(congruent_shift_from_no_prime),
    same_prime_sentence_as_scored == TRUE
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    prime_condition = factor(prime_condition, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  ) %>%
  group_by(model_condition, lexicality_condition, prime_condition) %>%
  mutate(
    prime_surprisal_z = as.numeric(scale(prime_surprisal_mean))
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp3_ife <- lmerTest::lmer(
  congruent_shift_from_no_prime ~
    prime_surprisal_z * prime_condition * lexicality_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp3_ife_clean,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

summary(m_exp3_ife)
drop1(m_exp3_ife, test = "F")
```

```{r}
ife_exp3_slopes <- emmeans::emtrends(
  m_exp3_ife,
  ~ prime_condition * lexicality_condition,
  var = "prime_surprisal_z"
)

ife_exp3_slopes
test(ife_exp3_slopes, null = 0)
```

NO IFE!!

In Exp3, prime surprisal predicted congruent priming shifts, but not in a uniform IFE-compatible direction. Active primes showed a small positive surprisal effect: more surprising active primes produced stronger active-congruent shifts. Passive primes, however, showed the opposite pattern: higher passive-prime surprisal predicted weaker passive-congruent shifts, with the strongest negative slope in jabberwocky. Thus, although Exp3 showed clear passive priming, this passive priming was not explained by an inverse-frequency/error-driven account. Instead, passive priming appears stronger when passive primes are more fluently encoded, consistent with a residual structural-trace or mapping-fluency interpretation.

# Experiment 4: Comprehension / Role Recovery

Question: does priming or target voice affect role-recovery accuracy?

Primary DV: correct answer to doer / acted-on question. Experiment 4 scoring is referent-head based: a response can be correct with the head noun alone or a larger NP containing that head, provided the foil head is absent. Inspect `matched_label` rates (`correct`, `foil`, `ambiguous`, `other`) alongside binary accuracy so role reversals are not hidden.

```{r}
# Load data and sanity checks
exp4_comprehension <- read_optional_csv(
  path_from_param(params$exp4_comprehension_path),
  "Experiment 4 comprehension item-level"
) %>%
  canonicalize_common()

exp4_pe <- read_optional_csv(
  path_from_param(params$exp4_pe_path),
  "Experiment 4 Sinclair PE item-level"
) %>%
  canonicalize_common()

exp4_comprehension %>%
  count(
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()

exp4_comprehension %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    missing_is_correct = sum(is.na(is_correct)),
    mean_accuracy = mean(is_correct, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()

exp4_pe %>%
  count(
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()

exp4_pe %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    missing_pe_mean = sum(is.na(pe_passive_minus_active_mean)),
    missing_pe_sum = sum(is.na(pe_passive_minus_active_sum))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

RQ1: does priming or target voice affect role-recovery accuracy? H1: Passives may be predictable as strings but harder as event-role mappings.

```{r}
# Prepare main comprehension dataframe
exp4_comp <- exp4_comprehension %>%
  filter(!is.na(is_correct)) %>%
  mutate(
    is_correct = as.integer(is_correct),
    target_voice = factor(target_voice, levels = c("active", "passive")),
    prime_condition = factor(
      prime_condition,
      levels = c("no_prime", "filler", "active", "passive")
    ),
    lexicality_condition = factor(lexicality_condition),
    complexity_condition = factor(complexity_condition),
    question_type = factor(question_type),
    model_condition = factor(model_condition),
    item_factor = factor(item_factor)
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

exp4_comp %>%
  count(
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    prime_condition,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition
  ) %>%
  arrange(
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    prime_condition
  ) %>%
  print(n = 50)
```

#### baseline comprehension/role recovery

RQ: Before priming, how well do models recover event roles from active and passive targets? H: we hypothesis a divergence between passive predictability (easier; higher summed/mean probabilities) and passive thematic-role recovering (lower than active overall). For the latter our prediction is nuanced: we expect easier agent recovery in both actives (=grammatical subject) and passives (the by-phrase is a strong cue) when lexico-semantics is present; when it is not (jabberwocky) we expect the expect lower accuracy for the agent in the active and higher in the passive. Then we expect easier patient recovering in the actives but not in the passive.

Question Prediction doer / agent = active should be poor, because the model lacks semantic support for identifying the agent; 
-\> confirmed doer / agent = passive should be better because the by-phrase explicitly marks the doer 
-\> confirmed acted-on / patient = active should be better than passive; 
-\> confirmed acted-on / patient = passive patient recovery should be poor because the patient-as-subject mapping is less semantically supported -\> confirmed

```{r}
exp4_baseline <- exp4_comp %>%
  filter(prime_condition == "no_prime") %>%
  droplevels()

exp4_baseline %>%
  group_by(
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    accuracy = mean(is_correct),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3)

exp4_baseline %>%
  group_by(
    #model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    accuracy = mean(is_correct),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(lexicality_condition, target_voice) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3)
```

- CORE: Active accuracy is higher when the question asks about the doer, especially when the patient is complex. But active acted_on questions are around chance or below. Passive doer questions are relatively better. -\> accuracy depends heavily on question type and which role has to be recovered.
- JABBER: In jabberwocky, active comprehension is very poor, especially for doer questions. Passive acted_on is also very poor but passive doer remains much higher. -\> It suggests that in jabberwocky, models are not generally recovering event roles. Instead, they may be relying on surface heuristics or question-specific cues.

```{r}
exp4_baseline_agg <- exp4_baseline %>%
  filter(model_condition != "9B_base") %>%
  group_by(
    item_factor,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    correct = sum(is_correct),
    total = n(),
    incorrect = total - correct,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp4_baseline <- lme4::glmer(
  cbind(correct, incorrect) ~
    target_voice * lexicality_condition * question_type +
    complexity_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp4_baseline_agg,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)


drop1(m_exp4_baseline, test = "Chisq")
```

- So in CORE, stronger models do better, and active is often easier than passive, especially for base models. But 3B_instruct and 8B_instruct are fairly balanced.

- In jabberwocky, passive accuracy is consistently higher than active accuracy for the non-9B models. Event-role recovery is difficult overall, and passive comprehension is not uniformly worse. Instead, passive comprehension depends strongly on question type and lexicality. In jabberwocky, passive sentences may even produce higher accuracy than active sentences because passive morphology and the by-phrase provide overt role cues.

```{r}
summary(m_exp4_baseline)
```

Estimated baseline accuracy

```{r}
emm_exp4_baseline <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_baseline,
  ~ target_voice | lexicality_condition * question_type,
  type = "response"
)

emm_exp4_baseline
```

active/passive baseline contrast

```{r}
pairs(
  emm_exp4_baseline,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "question_type"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

comprehension accuracy depends jointly on:

- voice of the target sentence
- lexicality
- which role the question asks for

Baseline comprehension accuracy showed a strong target voice × lexicality × question type interaction. \* In CORE, active targets were more accurate than passive targets for acted-on questions (patient recovery), but active and passive targets did not differ for doer questions (agent recovery). \* In jabberwocky, the pattern diverged sharply: active targets were more accurate for acted-on questions (direct object), whereas passive targets were dramatically more accurate for doer questions (by-phrase cue).

##### aggregated results (not by model)
```{r}
# Aggregate baseline role-recovery estimates across model conditions
# and complexity conditions
emm_exp4_baseline_agg <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_baseline,
  ~ target_voice * lexicality_condition * question_type,
  type = "response",
  weights = "equal"
) %>%
  as.data.frame()

emm_exp4_baseline_agg
```
```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)

pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.35)

ggplot(
  emm_exp4_baseline_agg,
  aes(
    x = target_voice,
    y = prob,
    colour = lexicality_condition,
    group = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0.5,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.6,
    colour = "grey45"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = asymp.LCL,
      ymax = asymp.UCL
    ),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7,
    position = pd
  ) +
  geom_point(
    size = 3.5,
    position = pd
  ) +
  geom_line(
    linewidth = 0.7,
    position = pd
  ) +
  facet_wrap(
    ~ question_type,
    labeller = labeller(
      question_type = c(
        "doer" = "Doer / agent recovery",
        "acted_on" = "Acted-on / patient recovery"
      )
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2),
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 4: baseline role-recovery accuracy",
    subtitle = paste(
      "No-prime condition; estimates aggregated across model",
      "and complexity conditions"
    ),
    x = "Target voice",
    y = "Estimated probability of correct role recovery",
    colour = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )
```


#### baseline-centred comprehension priming effect

RQ: Does an active or passive prime move comprehension accuracy away from the no-prime baseline? H: If structural priming supports role recovery, congruent primes should improve accuracy relative to no-prime.

```{r}
# prepare baseline-centred data
exp4_comp_analysis <- exp4_comp %>%
  filter(
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active", "passive"),
    target_voice %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(is_correct)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    is_correct = as.integer(is_correct),
    prime_condition_chr = as.character(prime_condition),
    target_voice_chr = as.character(target_voice),

    prime_relation = dplyr::case_when(
      prime_condition_chr == "no_prime" ~ "no_prime",
      prime_condition_chr == target_voice_chr ~ "congruent",
      prime_condition_chr != target_voice_chr ~ "incongruent",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),

    prime_relation = factor(
      prime_relation,
      levels = c("no_prime", "incongruent", "congruent")
    ),

    prime_condition = factor(
      prime_condition_chr,
      levels = c("no_prime", "active", "passive")
    ),
    target_voice = factor(target_voice_chr, levels = c("active", "passive")),
    lexicality_condition = factor(lexicality_condition),
    question_type = factor(question_type),
    complexity_condition = factor(
      complexity_condition,
      levels = c("agent_complex", "patient_complex", "both_complex")
    ),
    model_condition = factor(model_condition),
    item_factor = factor(item_factor)
  ) %>%
  select(-prime_condition_chr, -target_voice_chr) %>%
  filter(!is.na(prime_relation)) %>%
  droplevels()

exp4_comp_analysis %>%
  count(prime_condition, target_voice, prime_relation) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

```{r}
exp4_comp_shift_cell <- exp4_comp_analysis %>%
  group_by(
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition,
    prime_relation
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    correct = sum(is_correct),
    total = n(),
    incorrect = total - correct,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

m_exp4_comp_shift <- glm(
  cbind(correct, incorrect) ~
    prime_relation * target_voice * lexicality_condition * question_type +
    complexity_condition +
    model_condition,
  data = exp4_comp_shift_cell,
  family = binomial()
)


drop1(m_exp4_comp_shift_fast, test = "Chisq")

```

```{r}
summary(m_exp4_comp_shift)
```

Estimated accuracy by prime relation

```{r}
emm_exp4_comp_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_comp_shift,
  ~ prime_relation | lexicality_condition * target_voice * question_type,
  type = "response"
)

emm_exp4_comp_shift
```

Baseline-centered contrasts

```{r}
emm_exp4_comp_shift_link <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_comp_shift,
  ~ prime_relation | lexicality_condition * target_voice * question_type,
  type = "link"
)

exp4_comp_baseline_contrasts <- contrast(
  emm_exp4_comp_shift_link,
  method = list(
    "incongruent - no_prime"  = c(-1, 1, 0),
    "congruent - no_prime"    = c(-1, 0, 1),
    "congruent - incongruent" = c(0, -1, 1)
  ),
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "target_voice", "question_type"),
  adjust = "holm"
)

exp4_comp_baseline_contrasts
```
CORE ACTIVE: 

* patient qs: no priming effect
* agent qs: no priming effect

CORE PASSIVE:

* patient qs: Passive congruency helps relative to an incongruent prime, but not relative to no prime.
* agent qs: ""

JABBERWOCKY ACTIVE:

* patient qs: Both primes hurt relative to baseline, but congruent hurts less.
* agent qs: Both primes improve over baseline, and congruent improves more.
Active congruent primes help doer recovery, but not acted-on recovery. For acted-on questions, the prime context disrupts performance, although congruent structure is less disruptive than incongruent structure.

JABBERWOCKY PASSIVE:
* patient qs: Both primes strongly improve comprehension, and congruent improves more.
* agent qs: Both primes hurt comprehension, and congruent passive primes hurt more.
For jabberwocky passive targets, priming helps patient/acted-on recovery but harms doer recovery. Congruent passive primes are especially harmful for doer questions.

##### aggregated results
```{r}
ci_low_col <- intersect(
  c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL", "LCL"),
  names(exp4_comp_baseline_plot_df)
)[1]

ci_high_col <- intersect(
  c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL", "UCL"),
  names(exp4_comp_baseline_plot_df)
)[1]

if (is.na(ci_low_col) || is.na(ci_high_col)) {
  stop("Could not identify confidence-interval columns.")
}

exp4_comp_baseline_plot_df <- exp4_comp_baseline_plot_df %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low = .data[[ci_low_col]],
    ci_high = .data[[ci_high_col]],

    contrast = factor(
      contrast,
      levels = c(
        "Incongruent − no prime",
        "Congruent − no prime"
      )
    ),

    target_voice = factor(
      target_voice,
      levels = c("active", "passive")
    )
  )

pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.45)

ggplot(
  exp4_comp_baseline_plot_df,
  aes(
    x = target_voice,
    y = estimate,
    colour = contrast,
    group = contrast
  )
) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.6,
    colour = "grey40"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = ci_low,
      ymax = ci_high
    ),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7,
    position = pd
  ) +
  geom_point(
    size = 3.4,
    position = pd
  ) +
  geom_line(
    linewidth = 0.7,
    position = pd
  ) +
  facet_grid(
    question_type ~ lexicality_condition,
    labeller = labeller(
      lexicality_condition = c(
        core = "Core",
        jabberwocky = "Jabberwocky"
      ),
      question_type = c(
        doer = "Doer / agent recovery",
        acted_on = "Acted-on / patient recovery"
      )
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 4: baseline-centred comprehension priming",
    subtitle = paste(
      "Estimated change in role-recovery accuracy relative to no prime;",
      "aggregated across models and complexity conditions"
    ),
    x = "Target voice",
    y = "Change in probability of correct role recovery",
    colour = "Prime contrast"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```
```{r}
exp4_congruent_baseline_plot_df <-
  exp4_comp_baseline_plot_df %>%
  filter(contrast == "Congruent − no prime")

pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.35)

ggplot(
  exp4_congruent_baseline_plot_df,
  aes(
    x = target_voice,
    y = estimate,
    colour = lexicality_condition,
    group = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.6,
    colour = "grey40"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = ci_low,
      ymax = ci_high
    ),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7,
    position = pd
  ) +
  geom_point(
    size = 3.5,
    position = pd
  ) +
  geom_line(
    linewidth = 0.7,
    position = pd
  ) +
  facet_wrap(
    ~ question_type,
    labeller = labeller(
      question_type = c(
        doer = "Doer / agent recovery",
        acted_on = "Acted-on / patient recovery"
      )
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 4: congruent-prime shift from baseline",
    subtitle = paste(
      "Congruent prime minus no-prime accuracy;",
      "aggregated across models and complexity conditions"
    ),
    x = "Target voice",
    y = "Change in probability of correct role recovery",
    colour = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

#### The role of complexity

RQ: Does priming help more when the sentence has more role-mapping load?

H: If complexity increases role-recovery difficulty, congruent primes may help more in complex conditions. However, priming may also reverse if structural repetition does not support the queried role.

```{r}
exp4_congruency_only_df <- exp4_comp_complex_df %>%
  filter(contrast == "congruent - incongruent") %>%
  mutate(
    complexity_condition = factor(
      complexity_condition,
      levels = c("agent_complex", "patient_complex", "both_complex")
    )
  )

ggplot(
  exp4_congruency_only_df,
  aes(
    x = complexity_condition,
    y = estimate,
    color = target_voice,
    group = target_voice
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(width = 0.35)) +
  facet_grid(question_type ~ lexicality_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 4: comprehension priming by complexity",
    subtitle = "Variable plotted: congruent - incongruent. Positive = matching prime improves role recovery.",
    x = "Complexity condition",
    y = "Comprehension priming effect, log-odds scale",
    color = "Target voice"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```
complexity levels do not qualitatively change the priming pattern. Priming helps or hurts depending on which role has to be recovered.

```{r}
emm_exp4_shift_simple_link <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_comp_shift_fast,
  ~ prime_relation | lexicality_condition * target_voice * question_type,
  type = "link"
)

exp4_congruent_baseline_simple <- contrast(
  emm_exp4_shift_simple_link,
  method = list(
    "congruent - no_prime" = c(-1, 0, 1)
  ),
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "target_voice", "question_type"),
  adjust = "holm"
)

exp4_congruent_baseline_simple_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(exp4_congruent_baseline_simple, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

ci_low_col <- intersect(
  c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL", "LCL"),
  names(exp4_congruent_baseline_simple_df)
)[1]

ci_high_col <- intersect(
  c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL", "UCL"),
  names(exp4_congruent_baseline_simple_df)
)[1]

exp4_congruent_baseline_simple_df <- exp4_congruent_baseline_simple_df %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low = .data[[ci_low_col]],
    ci_high = .data[[ci_high_col]]
  )

ggplot(
  exp4_congruent_baseline_simple_df,
  aes(
    x = target_voice,
    y = estimate,
    color = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ question_type) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 4: congruent-prime shift from no-prime baseline",
    subtitle = "Variable plotted: congruent - no_prime. Positive = matching prime improves role recovery.",
    x = "Target voice",
    y = "Shift in comprehension accuracy, log-odds scale",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```
* When lexical-semantic information is available, the model may already use the event content to recover roles. The prime has less room to help, and in some cases the extra prime context slightly disrupts comprehension.

* without lexical semantics, the prime matters much more. But whether it helps depends on which role is being queried.

Passive structures can be predictable and strongly primed at the surface-structural level, especially without lexical semantics, but this does not guarantee robust thematic-role recovery.

#### comprehension independent priming effect

RQ: Does Exp4 show structural priming in probability space, independent of comprehension? H1. baseline preference: we expect higher passive preference as in experiment 1b and 3 given passives structural predictability 2. priming shift: we expect balanced priming effect

```{r}
exp4_pe_baseline <- exp4_pe %>%
  filter(
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    lexicality_condition %in% c("core", "jabberwocky")
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp4_pe_baseline <- lmerTest::lmer(
  baseline_no_prime_passive_minus_active_logprob_mean ~
    lexicality_condition * complexity_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp4_pe_baseline,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp4_pe_baseline, test = "F")

```

```{r}
summary(m_exp4_pe_baseline)
```

Interpretation:

positive = passive target scored higher than active target 
negative = active target scored higher than passive target

Estimated PE baseline:

```{r}
emm_exp4_pe_baseline <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_pe_baseline,
  ~ lexicality_condition * complexity_condition
)

emm_exp4_pe_baseline

test(emm_exp4_pe_baseline, null = 0)
```
* passive targets are consistently more predictable than active targets at the no-prime baseline.
* Adding complexity reduces the passive scoring advantage, especially when both roles are complex, but the passive target remains more probable than the active target in every condition.
* Jabberwocky slightly reduces the passive scoring advantage in agent_complex and patient_complex but in both_complex, core and jabberwocky are essentially identical
So lexicality matters, but only modestly compared with the overall passive advantage.

Passive targets are highly predictable in the scoring/PE measure, even though passive role comprehension is not uniformly better.

the scoring system is not simply measuring “better comprehension.” It is capturing a strong surface-form / morphosyntactic predictability advantage for passives.

#### PE baseline-centered target-congruent shifts

```{r}
exp4_pe_shift_long <- exp4_pe %>%
  filter(
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    lexicality_condition %in% c("core", "jabberwocky")
  ) %>%
  select(
    item_factor,
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    complexity_condition,
    active_target_shift_active_minus_no_prime_mean,
    passive_target_shift_passive_minus_no_prime_mean
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      active_target_shift_active_minus_no_prime_mean,
      passive_target_shift_passive_minus_no_prime_mean
    ),
    names_to = "target_voice",
    values_to = "pe_shift_from_no_prime",
    names_pattern = "(active|passive)_target_shift_.*_minus_no_prime_mean"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    target_voice = factor(target_voice, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp4_pe_shift <- lmerTest::lmer(
  pe_shift_from_no_prime ~
    target_voice * lexicality_condition * complexity_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp4_pe_shift_long,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp4_pe_shift, test = "F")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp4_pe_shift)
```

Interpretation:

active row = active prime effect on active target relative to no-prime 
passive row = passive prime effect on passive target relative to no-prime 
positive = target-congruent scoring improved relative to baseline

Estimated PE shift:

```{r}
emm_exp4_pe_shift <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_pe_shift,
  ~ target_voice | lexicality_condition * complexity_condition
)

emm_exp4_pe_shift

test(emm_exp4_pe_shift, null = 0)
```

```{r}
emm_exp4_pe_shift_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(emm_exp4_pe_shift, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

ci_low_col <- intersect(
  c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL", "LCL"),
  names(emm_exp4_pe_shift_df)
)[1]

ci_high_col <- intersect(
  c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL", "UCL"),
  names(emm_exp4_pe_shift_df)
)[1]

emm_exp4_pe_shift_df <- emm_exp4_pe_shift_df %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low = .data[[ci_low_col]],
    ci_high = .data[[ci_high_col]]
  )

ggplot(
  emm_exp4_pe_shift_df,
  aes(
    x = complexity_condition,
    y = emmean,
    color = target_voice,
    group = target_voice
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.35), size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(width = 0.35)) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicality_condition) +
  labs(
    title = "Experiment 4: PE shifts from no-prime baseline",
    subtitle = "Variable plotted: pe_shift_from_no_prime. Positive = target-congruent scoring improved.",
    x = "Complexity condition",
    y = "PE shift from no-prime baseline",
    color = "Target voice"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```
* Both active and passive primes reliably increase the probability of the matching target relative to baseline.
* Jabberwocky has much larger PE shifts than CORE.
* real-language active targets get the larger target-congruent probability boost.
* under semantic impoverishment, passive structural repetition gets the larger probability boost.

```{r}
pairs(
  emm_exp4_pe_shift,
  by = c("lexicality_condition", "complexity_condition"),
  adjust = "holm"
)
```
In real-language contexts, active mappings remain strongly supported by lexical/event semantics. In jabberwocky, passive surface cues become especially salient, so passive structural repetition gives a larger scoring boost.

#### PE-comprehension dissociation

RQ: Does structural probability priming predict comprehension improvement? H: If structural PE reflects genuine role-comprehension support, stronger congruent PE should predict larger comprehension benefit.

If probability priming and comprehension dissociate, PE will not reliably predict comprehension improvement, or the relationship will differ by target voice, lexicality, and question type.

```{r}
exp4_prime_for_dissociation <- exp4_comp %>%
  filter(
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    prime_condition %in% c("active", "passive"),
    target_voice %in% c("active", "passive"),
    !is.na(is_correct)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    is_correct = as.integer(is_correct),
    prime_condition_chr = as.character(prime_condition),
    target_voice_chr = as.character(target_voice),

    prime_congruent = prime_condition_chr == target_voice_chr,
    prime_congruent = factor(
      prime_congruent,
      levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
      labels = c("incongruent", "congruent")
    ),

    prime_condition = factor(prime_condition_chr, levels = c("active", "passive")),
    target_voice = factor(target_voice_chr, levels = c("active", "passive"))
  ) %>%
  select(-prime_condition_chr, -target_voice_chr) %>%
  droplevels()

exp4_comp_congruency <- exp4_prime_for_dissociation %>%
  group_by(
    item_factor,
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition,
    prime_congruent
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    correct = sum(is_correct),
    total = n(),
    accuracy = correct / total,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    elogit_accuracy = qlogis((correct + 0.5) / (total + 1))
  ) %>%
  select(
    item_factor,
    item_id,
    item_index,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    target_voice,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition,
    prime_congruent,
    accuracy,
    elogit_accuracy
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = prime_congruent,
    values_from = c(accuracy, elogit_accuracy)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    comprehension_congruency_effect =
      accuracy_congruent - accuracy_incongruent,

    comprehension_congruency_logodds =
      elogit_accuracy_congruent - elogit_accuracy_incongruent
  )
```

Prepare target-specific congruent PE:

```{r}
exp4_pe_congruent <- exp4_pe %>%
  filter(
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    lexicality_condition %in% c("core", "jabberwocky")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    item_index_key = as.character(item_index),
    model_condition = as.character(model_condition),
    lexicality_condition = as.character(lexicality_condition),
    complexity_condition = as.character(complexity_condition)
  ) %>%
  select(
    item_index_key,
    model_condition,
    lexicality_condition,
    complexity_condition,
    pe_active_target_logprob_mean_same_minus_other,
    pe_passive_target_logprob_mean_same_minus_other,
    pe_active_target_logprob_sum_same_minus_other,
    pe_passive_target_logprob_sum_same_minus_other
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      pe_active_target_logprob_mean_same_minus_other,
      pe_passive_target_logprob_mean_same_minus_other,
      pe_active_target_logprob_sum_same_minus_other,
      pe_passive_target_logprob_sum_same_minus_other
    ),
    names_to = c("target_voice", ".value"),
    names_pattern = "pe_(active|passive)_target_logprob_(mean|sum)_same_minus_other"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    congruent_pe_mean = mean,
    congruent_pe_sum = sum
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    target_voice = as.character(target_voice)
  ) %>%
  distinct()

exp4_pe_congruent %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    missing_pe_mean = sum(is.na(congruent_pe_mean)),
    missing_pe_sum = sum(is.na(congruent_pe_sum)),
    mean_pe = mean(congruent_pe_mean, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

Join PE and comprehension:

```{r}
exp4_comp_congruency_keyed <- exp4_comp_congruency %>%
  mutate(
    item_index_key = as.character(item_index),
    model_condition = as.character(model_condition),
    lexicality_condition = as.character(lexicality_condition),
    complexity_condition = as.character(complexity_condition),
    target_voice = as.character(target_voice)
  )

exp4_pe_comp_compare <- exp4_comp_congruency_keyed %>%
  left_join(
    exp4_pe_congruent,
    by = c(
      "item_index_key",
      "model_condition",
      "lexicality_condition",
      "complexity_condition",
      "target_voice"
    )
  )

exp4_pe_comp_compare %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    missing_pe = sum(is.na(congruent_pe_mean)),
    nonmissing_pe = sum(!is.na(congruent_pe_mean)),
    mean_comp_effect = mean(comprehension_congruency_effect, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_pe = mean(congruent_pe_mean, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

```{r}
exp4_pe_comp_compare_model <- exp4_pe_comp_compare %>%
  group_by(model_condition, lexicality_condition, target_voice) %>%
  mutate(
    congruent_pe_z = as.numeric(scale(congruent_pe_mean))
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(comprehension_congruency_logodds),
    !is.na(congruent_pe_z)
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

m_exp4_pe_comp <- lmerTest::lmer(
  comprehension_congruency_logodds ~
    congruent_pe_z * target_voice * lexicality_condition * question_type +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp4_pe_comp_compare_model,
  REML = FALSE,
  control = lme4::lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp4_pe_comp, test = "F")
```
Within each condition, do items/models with stronger structural PE also show stronger comprehension priming?

```{r}
summary(m_exp4_pe_comp)
```

Slopes:

```{r}
pe_comp_slopes <- emmeans::emtrends(
  m_exp4_pe_comp,
  ~ target_voice * lexicality_condition * question_type,
  var = "congruent_pe_z"
)

pe_comp_slopes

test(pe_comp_slopes, null = 0)
```

```{r}
pe_comp_slopes_df <- as.data.frame(
  summary(pe_comp_slopes, infer = c(TRUE, TRUE))
)

ci_low_col <- intersect(
  c("lower.CL", "asymp.LCL", "LCL"),
  names(pe_comp_slopes_df)
)[1]

ci_high_col <- intersect(
  c("upper.CL", "asymp.UCL", "UCL"),
  names(pe_comp_slopes_df)
)[1]

pe_comp_slopes_df <- pe_comp_slopes_df %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low = .data[[ci_low_col]],
    ci_high = .data[[ci_high_col]]
  )

ggplot(
  pe_comp_slopes_df,
  aes(
    x = target_voice,
    y = congruent_pe_z.trend,
    color = lexicality_condition
  )
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.35), size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ question_type) +
  labs(
    title = "Exp4: Does structural PE predict comprehension priming?",
    subtitle = "Variable plotted: congruent_pe_z.trend. Positive = stronger PE predicts larger comprehension benefit.",
    x = "Target voice",
    y = "Slope of congruent PE",
    color = "Lexicality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```
Even within the same condition, the magnitude of structural PE usually does not explain the magnitude of comprehension priming.

Whereas Sinclair et al. show that LM surprisal priming and human EEG priming only partially align, our study shows why that partial alignment may arise: LM priming can reflect surface morphosyntactic expectation rather than the integrated event-role representation that human comprehension ultimately requires.

## The role of semantics: eventive vs stative verbs
```{r}
# Pick eventivity columns robustly
eventivity_col_comp <- dplyr::case_when(
  "target_verb_eventivity" %in% names(exp4_comprehension) ~ "target_verb_eventivity",
  "verb_eventivity" %in% names(exp4_comprehension) ~ "verb_eventivity",
  TRUE ~ NA_character_
)

eventivity_col_pe <- dplyr::case_when(
  "target_verb_eventivity" %in% names(exp4_pe) ~ "target_verb_eventivity",
  "verb_eventivity" %in% names(exp4_pe) ~ "verb_eventivity",
  TRUE ~ NA_character_
)

stopifnot(!is.na(eventivity_col_comp))
stopifnot(!is.na(eventivity_col_pe))

```

```{r}
exp4_comp_ev_core <- exp4_comprehension %>%
  mutate(
    target_eventivity = .data[[eventivity_col_comp]],
    target_eventivity = factor(
      target_eventivity,
      levels = c("eventive", "stative")
    ),
    is_correct = as.integer(is_correct),
    target_voice = factor(target_voice, levels = c("active", "passive")),
    prime_condition = factor(
      prime_condition,
      levels = c("no_prime", "filler", "active", "passive")
    ),
    lexicality_condition = factor(lexicality_condition),
    question_type = factor(question_type),
    complexity_condition = factor(
      complexity_condition,
      levels = c("agent_complex", "patient_complex", "both_complex")
    ),
    model_condition = factor(model_condition),
    item_factor = factor(item_factor)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    lexicality_condition == "core",
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    !is.na(target_eventivity),
    target_eventivity %in% c("eventive", "stative")
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

exp4_pe_ev_core <- exp4_pe %>%
  mutate(
    target_eventivity = .data[[eventivity_col_pe]],
    target_eventivity = factor(
      target_eventivity,
      levels = c("eventive", "stative")
    ),
    lexicality_condition = factor(lexicality_condition),
    complexity_condition = factor(
      complexity_condition,
      levels = c("agent_complex", "patient_complex", "both_complex")
    ),
    model_condition = factor(model_condition),
    item_factor = factor(item_factor)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    lexicality_condition == "core",
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    !is.na(target_eventivity),
    target_eventivity %in% c("eventive", "stative")
  ) %>%
  droplevels()
```

RQ4.5: Do stative and eventive passives differ in baseline role recovery?
H: Based on Paolazzi et al 2022, stative passives should be harder than eventive
```{r}
exp4_passive_baseline_ev <- exp4_comp_ev_core %>%
  filter(
    model_condition != "9B_base",
    target_voice == "passive",
    prime_condition == "no_prime",
    lexicality_condition == "core",
    !is.na(is_correct)
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

exp4_passive_baseline_ev %>%
  group_by(
    target_eventivity,
    lexicality_condition,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    accuracy = mean(is_correct),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3)

exp4_passive_baseline_ev %>%
  count(
    target_eventivity,
    lexicality_condition,
    question_type,
    matched_label
  ) %>%
  group_by(target_eventivity, lexicality_condition, question_type) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3)

exp4_passive_baseline_ev_agg <- exp4_passive_baseline_ev %>%
  group_by(
    item_factor,
    model_condition,
    target_eventivity,
    lexicality_condition,
    question_type,
    complexity_condition
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    correct = sum(is_correct),
    total = n(),
    incorrect = total - correct,
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

```{r}
m_exp4_passive_baseline_ev <- lme4::glmer(
  cbind(correct, incorrect) ~
    target_eventivity * question_type +
    complexity_condition +
    model_condition +
    (1 | item_factor),
  data = exp4_passive_baseline_ev_agg,
  family = binomial,
  control = lme4::glmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 2e5)
  )
)

drop1(m_exp4_passive_baseline_ev, test = "Chisq")
```

```{r}
summary(m_exp4_passive_baseline_ev)
```

```{r}
emm_exp4_passive_baseline_ev <- emmeans::emmeans(
  m_exp4_passive_baseline_ev,
  ~ target_eventivity | question_type,
  type = "response"
)

emm_exp4_passive_baseline_ev

pairs(
  emm_exp4_passive_baseline_ev,
  by = "question_type",
  adjust = "holm"
)
```

RQ4.6: Do stative and eventive passives differ in passive comprehension priming?

```{r}
exp4_passive_shift_ev_core <- exp4_comp_ev_core %>%
  filter(
    target_voice == "passive",
    prime_condition %in% c("no_prime", "active", "passive"),
    !is.na(is_correct)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    prime_relation = dplyr::case_when(
      as.character(prime_condition) == "no_prime" ~ "no_prime",
      as.character(prime_condition) == "passive" ~ "congruent",
      as.character(prime_condition) == "active" ~ "incongruent",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    prime_relation = factor(
      prime_relation,
      levels = c("no_prime", "incongruent", "congruent")
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(prime_relation)) %>%
  droplevels()
```



# Interpretation Checklist

Before writing claims:

1.  Baseline bias must be reported before priming effects.
2.  Priming should be reported as a shift away from no-prime baseline.
3.  Filler baseline should be shown as a robustness comparison.
4.  CORE/Jabberwocky differences should be interpreted only after token-count controls.
5.  `model_condition` is the primary model-comparison variable so base and instruct models remain distinct.
6.  `model_size`, `model_family`, and instruction tuning should be descriptive or robustness-only unless explicitly tested.
7.  ROI results should say whether effects happen at frame onset or later passive-cue regions.
8.  IFE results should be framed as evidence for or against surprisal-linked adaptation, not assumed.
